comment_text.sn35.132:1.1: Mahākaccāna was the first major Buddhist figure in the region, and evidently his presence challenged the local brahmins. The fact that he was a brahmin by birth was salt on the wound, prompting a jibe by the callow students.
comment_text.sn35.132:1.2: A certain brahmin Lohicca also appears in DN 12. But since this discourse is set far away in Avantī after the Buddha’s passing, and since in both cases he is said to have taken refuge, it seems likely these are different people. | The term seleyyaka (variant selissaka) is unique and its meaning unclear. The commentary explains it as a kind of game like leapfrog, but contextually the sense “jeer” is more cohesive. Perhaps it is related to Sanskrit śālaka, “jester, buffon”.
comment_text.sn35.132:1.3: Bharataka here is unique and its sense debatable. The commentary says they were local householders; but the context is the intrusion of Buddhism into territory dominated by Vedic ritualism. In the Rig Veda, the Bharatas were the clan ruled by King Sudās, who, blessed by Agni, the “fire of the Bharatas”, and allied with the Vāsiṣṭhas, overcame the armies of ten kings to dominate the Aryan coalition. Wijesekera has argued that bhara in this context originally meant the “booty” or “plunder” gained by raiding villages and towns (Buddhist and Vedic Studies, ch. 19). A bhārata was a descendant of the tribe, and with their cultural ascendance became a term for all Vedists. Later it became generalized; the Jain Triṣaṣṭiśalākāpuruṣacaritra of the 12th century says that Bhāratakas include all Indians, whether Aryan or not (3.29). The Pali form bharataka could here be simply a variation of bhārata in the sense “Aryan”, but the suffix -ka is often diminutive if not contemptuous (eg. samaṇaka, muṇḍaka). Compare Rig Veda 3.53.24, which appears to criticize false Bhāratas as betrayers of Vedic culture.
comment_text.sn35.132:1.8: Similar accounts of pure, meditative Brahmans of the past and their present fallen state are at Snp 2.7 and DN 27:22.1. Buddhists argued that the real brahmins lived much like Buddhist mendicants.
comment_text.sn35.132:2.2: Purāṇa (“ancient”) is familiar as the name for the class of lengthy scriptures that gather legends and other forms of the traditional knowledge. As we have them today, they have undergone extensive development, but they draw on ancient sources. In Pali these are normally called itihāsa (“testaments”; see note on MN 91:2.1), and elsewhere we find purāṇa in this sense only in the late canon (Mil 5.3.7:6.1). However, pre-Buddhist texts refer to itihāsa along with purāṇa  (Atharva Veda 15.6.10, Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 11.5.6.8, Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 2.4.10, Chāndogya Upaniṣad 3.4.1). Given the context, it is likely this is the meaning here.
comment_text.sn35.132:4.1: Japāmase is middle plural imperative. The sense is that they prioritize recitation as their core duty while neglecting conduct.
comment_text.sn35.132:4.3: The true brahmin does not take up arms (Snp 3.9:42.2, Dhp 406).
comment_text.sn35.132:4.4: PTS and BJT read virajjhamānā tasathāvaresu (“attacking both the strong and the weak”). This appears to be a normalization, as the commentary supports MS virajjamānā sataṇhātaṇhesu. The point is that in their rage they did not even spare holy men.
comment_text.sn35.132:5.3: Compare the longer description of Vedic rites at MN 51:10.3. | “Fasting” (anāsakā) was undertaken in preparation for the ritual (Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 9.5.1.6). | The thaṇḍila (Sanskrit sthaṇḍila) was the ground cleared behind the fire altar (Chāndogya Upaniṣad 5.2.7, Śāṅkhāyana Āraṇyaka 9.8). The sponsor, having performed the ritual, lies on the bare ground or on a hide. If he sees a woman in his dreams, he knows his wish will be fulfilled.
comment_text.sn35.132:5.4: Pāto sinānaṁ (“bathing at daybreak”) is recorded as a Brahmanical practice also at SN 7.21:1.5; bathing three times is one of the austere practices (MN 12:45.12; cf. Kd 1:20.15.1). A brahmin was to bathe at dawn and evening before reciting the Gāyatrī (Manu 4.93, Garuḍa Purāṇa 229).
comment_text.sn35.132:6.1: The hide was typically that of the black antelope. | “Mud” seems out of place, as we have already seen they are concerned with bathing, and going unwashed was a Jain practice. But perhaps it is deliberately incoherent.
comment_text.sn35.132:6.3: “Fawning” (kuhana) is using dissembling and blandishments to obtain favor from the wealthy.
comment_text.sn35.132:6.4: Before and after rituals and meal, a little water was cupped in the hand and sipped.
comment_text.sn35.132:6.6: See Bodhi Connected Discourses for a note on this line.

root_text.sn35.132:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ āyasmā mahākaccāno avantīsu viharati makkarakate araññakuṭikāyaṁ.
root_text.sn35.132:1.2: Atha kho lohiccassa brāhmaṇassa sambahulā antevāsikā kaṭṭhahārakā māṇavakā yenāyasmato mahākaccānassa araññakuṭikā tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā parito parito kuṭikāya anucaṅkamanti anuvicaranti uccāsaddā mahāsaddā kānici kānici seleyyakāni karonti:
root_text.sn35.132:1.3: “ime pana muṇḍakā samaṇakā ibbhā kaṇhā bandhupādāpaccā, imesaṁ bharatakānaṁ sakkatā garukatā mānitā pūjitā apacitā”ti.
root_text.sn35.132:1.4: Atha kho āyasmā mahākaccāno vihārā nikkhamitvā te māṇavake etadavoca:
root_text.sn35.132:1.7: Evaṁ vutte, te māṇavakā tuṇhī ahesuṁ.
root_text.sn35.132:1.8: Atha kho āyasmā mahākaccāno te māṇavake gāthāhi ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn35.132:3.1: Dhamme ca jhāne ca ratā ahesuṁ,
root_text.sn35.132:8.1: Atha kho te māṇavakā kupitā anattamanā yena lohicco brāhmaṇo tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā lohiccaṁ brāhmaṇaṁ etadavocuṁ:
root_text.sn35.132:8.2: “yagghe bhavaṁ jāneyya, samaṇo mahākaccāno brāhmaṇānaṁ mante ekaṁsena apavadati, paṭikkosatī”ti?
root_text.sn35.132:8.3: Evaṁ vutte, lohicco brāhmaṇo kupito ahosi anattamano.
root_text.sn35.132:8.5: “na kho pana metaṁ patirūpaṁ yohaṁ aññadatthu māṇavakānaṁyeva sutvā samaṇaṁ mahākaccānaṁ akkoseyyaṁ paribhāseyyaṁ.
root_text.sn35.132:9.1: Atha kho lohicco brāhmaṇo tehi māṇavakehi saddhiṁ yenāyasmā mahākaccāno tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā āyasmatā mahākaccānena saddhiṁ sammodi.
root_text.sn35.132:9.2: Sammodanīyaṁ kathaṁ sāraṇīyaṁ vītisāretvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho lohicco brāhmaṇo āyasmantaṁ mahākaccānaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn35.132:9.3: “āgamaṁsu nu khvidha, bho kaccāna, amhākaṁ sambahulā antevāsikā kaṭṭhahārakā māṇavakā”ti?
root_text.sn35.132:9.4: “Āgamaṁsu khvidha te, brāhmaṇa, sambahulā antevāsikā kaṭṭhahārakā māṇavakā”ti.
root_text.sn35.132:9.5: “Ahu pana bhoto kaccānassa tehi māṇavakehi saddhiṁ kocideva kathāsallāpo”ti?
root_text.sn35.132:9.6: “Ahu kho me, brāhmaṇa, tehi māṇavakehi saddhiṁ kocideva kathāsallāpo”ti.
root_text.sn35.132:9.7: “Yathā kathaṁ pana bhoto kaccānassa tehi māṇavakehi saddhiṁ ahosi kathāsallāpo”ti?
root_text.sn35.132:9.8: “Evaṁ kho me, brāhmaṇa, tehi māṇavakehi saddhiṁ ahosi kathāsallāpo:
root_text.sn35.132:11.1: Evaṁ kho me, brāhmaṇa, tehi māṇavakehi saddhiṁ ahosi kathāsallāpo”ti.
root_text.sn35.132:12.2: Kittāvatā nu kho, bho kaccāna, aguttadvāro hotī”ti?
root_text.sn35.132:12.3: “Idha, brāhmaṇa, ekacco cakkhunā rūpaṁ disvā piyarūpe rūpe adhimuccati, appiyarūpe rūpe byāpajjati, anupaṭṭhitakāyassati ca viharati, parittacetaso
root_text.sn35.132:12.4: tañca cetovimuttiṁ paññāvimuttiṁ yathābhūtaṁ nappajānāti yatthassa te uppannā pāpakā akusalā dhammā aparisesā nirujjhanti.
root_text.sn35.132:12.9: manasā dhammaṁ viññāya piyarūpe dhamme adhimuccati, appiyarūpe ca dhamme byāpajjati, anupaṭṭhitakāyassati ca viharati, parittacetaso
root_text.sn35.132:12.10: tañca cetovimuttiṁ paññāvimuttiṁ yathābhūtaṁ nappajānāti yatthassa te uppannā pāpakā akusalā dhammā aparisesā nirujjhanti.
root_text.sn35.132:12.13: Yāvañcidaṁ bhotā kaccānena aguttadvārova samāno aguttadvāroti akkhāto.
root_text.sn35.132:13.2: Kittāvatā nu kho, bho kaccāna, guttadvāro hotī”ti?
root_text.sn35.132:13.3: “Idha, brāhmaṇa, bhikkhu cakkhunā rūpaṁ disvā piyarūpe rūpe nādhimuccati, appiyarūpe rūpe na byāpajjati, upaṭṭhitakāyassati ca viharati, appamāṇacetaso
root_text.sn35.132:13.4: tañca cetovimuttiṁ paññāvimuttiṁ yathābhūtaṁ pajānāti yatthassa te uppannā pāpakā akusalā dhammā aparisesā nirujjhanti.
root_text.sn35.132:13.9: manasā dhammaṁ viññāya piyarūpe dhamme nādhimuccati, appiyarūpe dhamme na byāpajjati, upaṭṭhitakāyassati ca viharati, appamāṇacetaso
root_text.sn35.132:13.10: tañca cetovimuttiṁ paññāvimuttiṁ yathābhūtaṁ pajānāti, yatthassa te uppannā pāpakā akusalā dhammā aparisesā nirujjhanti.
root_text.sn35.132:14.2: Yāvañcidaṁ bhotā kaccānena guttadvārova samāno guttadvāroti akkhāto.
root_text.sn35.132:14.3: Abhikkantaṁ, bho kaccāna; abhikkantaṁ, bho kaccāna.
root_text.sn35.132:14.4: Seyyathāpi, bho kaccāna, nikkujjitaṁ vā ukkujjeyya, paṭicchannaṁ vā vivareyya, mūḷhassa vā maggaṁ ācikkheyya, andhakāre vā telapajjotaṁ dhāreyya: ‘cakkhumanto rūpāni dakkhantī’ti; evamevaṁ bhotā kaccānena anekapariyāyena dhammo pakāsito.
root_text.sn35.132:14.5: Esāhaṁ, bho kaccāna, taṁ bhagavantaṁ saraṇaṁ gacchāmi, dhammañca, bhikkhusaṅghañca.
root_text.sn35.132:14.6: Upāsakaṁ maṁ bhavaṁ kaccāno dhāretu ajjatagge pāṇupetaṁ saraṇaṁ gataṁ.
root_text.sn35.132:14.7: Yathā ca bhavaṁ kaccāno makkarakate upāsakakulāni upasaṅkamati; evameva lohiccakulaṁ upasaṅkamatu.
root_text.sn35.132:14.8: Tattha ye māṇavakā vā māṇavikā vā bhavantaṁ kaccānaṁ abhivādessanti paccuṭṭhissanti āsanaṁ vā udakaṁ vā dassanti, tesaṁ taṁ bhavissati dīgharattaṁ hitāya sukhāyā”ti.

translation_text.sn35.132:1.1: At one time Venerable Mahākaccāna was staying in the land of the Avantis in a wilderness hut near Makkarakaṭa.
translation_text.sn35.132:1.2: Then several students, pupils of the brahmin Lohicca, approached Mahākaccāna’s wilderness hut while collecting firewood. They walked and wandered all around the hut, making a colossal racket and all kinds of jeers:
translation_text.sn35.132:1.3: “These shavelings, fake ascetics, primitives, black spawn from the feet of our kinsman, the Lord! They’re honored, respected, revered, venerated, and esteemed by so-called inheritors of Vedic culture.”
translation_text.sn35.132:1.4: Then Mahākaccāna left his dwelling and said to those young students,
translation_text.sn35.132:1.6: I will speak to you on the teaching.”
translation_text.sn35.132:1.7: When this was said, the young students fell silent.
translation_text.sn35.132:1.8: Then Mahākaccāna recited these verses for them.
translation_text.sn35.132:2.3: Their sense doors were guarded, well protected,
translation_text.sn35.132:3.1: Those brahmins who remembered <j>the ancient legends
translation_text.sn35.132:4.1: But these have lost their way. <j>Saying, “We must recite”,
translation_text.sn35.132:4.2: they live out of balance, judging by clan.
translation_text.sn35.132:4.3: Mastered by anger, they take up many arms,
translation_text.sn35.132:4.4: indifferent to those with craving and without.
translation_text.sn35.132:5.1: All is vain for someone <j>who doesn’t guard the sense doors,
translation_text.sn35.132:5.2: like the wealth a person finds in a dream.
translation_text.sn35.132:5.4: bathing at daybreak, the three Vedas,
translation_text.sn35.132:6.2: hymns, precepts and observances, <j>and fervent austerities,
translation_text.sn35.132:6.6: are only used to generate profits.
translation_text.sn35.132:7.4: that’s the path to attainment of divinity!”
translation_text.sn35.132:8.1: Then those young students, offended and upset, went to the brahmin Lohicca and said to him,
translation_text.sn35.132:8.2: “Please, master, you should know this. The ascetic Mahākaccāna condemns and rejects outright the hymns of the brahmins!”
translation_text.sn35.132:8.3: When they said this, Lohicca was offended and upset.
translation_text.sn35.132:8.5: “But it wouldn’t be appropriate for me to abuse or insult the ascetic Mahākaccāna solely because of what I’ve heard from these young students.
translation_text.sn35.132:8.6: Why don’t I go and ask him about it?”
translation_text.sn35.132:9.1: Then the brahmin Lohicca together with those young students went to Venerable Mahākaccāna and exchanged greetings with him.
translation_text.sn35.132:9.2: When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to him,
translation_text.sn35.132:9.3: “Master Kaccāna, did several young pupils of mine come by here collecting firewood?”
translation_text.sn35.132:9.5: “But did you have some discussion with them?”
translation_text.sn35.132:9.7: “But what kind of discussion did you have with them?”
translation_text.sn35.132:9.8: “This is the discussion I had with these young students.” And he repeated the verses in full.
translation_text.sn35.132:12.1: “Worthy Kaccāna spoke of someone who doesn’t guard the sense doors.
translation_text.sn35.132:12.2: How do you define someone who doesn’t guard the sense doors?”
translation_text.sn35.132:12.3: “Brahmin, take someone who sees a sight with their eyes. If it’s pleasant they hold on to it, but if it’s unpleasant they dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body unestablished and their heart stunted.
translation_text.sn35.132:12.4: And they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without anything left over.
translation_text.sn35.132:12.5: When they hear a sound with their ears …
translation_text.sn35.132:12.6: When they smell an odor with their nose …
translation_text.sn35.132:12.7: When they taste a flavor with their tongue …
translation_text.sn35.132:12.8: When they feel a touch with their body …
translation_text.sn35.132:12.9: When they know an idea with their mind, if it’s pleasant they hold on to it, but if it’s unpleasant they dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body unestablished and a limited heart.
translation_text.sn35.132:12.10: And they don’t truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without anything left over.
translation_text.sn35.132:12.11: That’s how someone doesn’t guard the sense doors.”
translation_text.sn35.132:12.13: How accurately you’ve explained someone whose sense doors are unguarded!
translation_text.sn35.132:13.1: You also spoke of someone who does guard the sense doors.
translation_text.sn35.132:13.2: How do you define someone who does guard the sense doors?”
translation_text.sn35.132:13.3: “Brahmin, take a mendicant who sees a sight with their eyes. If it’s pleasant they don’t hold on to it, and if it’s unpleasant they don’t dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body established and a limitless heart.
translation_text.sn35.132:13.4: And they truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without anything left over.
translation_text.sn35.132:13.5: When they hear a sound with their ears …
translation_text.sn35.132:13.6: When they smell an odor with their nose …
translation_text.sn35.132:13.7: When they taste a flavor with their tongue …
translation_text.sn35.132:13.8: When they feel a touch with their body …
translation_text.sn35.132:13.9: When they know an idea with their mind, if it’s pleasant they don’t hold on to it, and if it’s unpleasant they don’t dislike it. They live with mindfulness of the body established and a limitless heart.
translation_text.sn35.132:13.10: And they truly understand the freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom where those arisen bad, unskillful qualities cease without anything left over.
translation_text.sn35.132:13.11: That’s how someone guards the sense doors.”
translation_text.sn35.132:14.2: How accurately you’ve explained someone whose sense doors are guarded!
translation_text.sn35.132:14.4: As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, the worthy Kaccāna has made the teaching clear in many ways.
translation_text.sn35.132:14.5: I go for refuge to the Buddha, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha.
translation_text.sn35.132:14.6: From this day forth, may the worthy Kaccāna remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.
translation_text.sn35.132:14.7: Please come to my family just as you go to the families of the lay followers in Makkarakaṭa.
translation_text.sn35.132:14.8: The brahmin boys and girls there will bow to you, rise in your presence, and give you a seat and water. That will be for their lasting welfare and happiness.”

variant_text.sn35.132:1.2: seleyyakāni karonti → selissakāni karontā (bj); selissakāni karonti (pts1ed)
variant_text.sn35.132:8.5: mahākaccānaṁ akkoseyyaṁ → akkoseyyaṁ virujjheyyaṁ (sya-all, km, mr)
variant_text.sn35.132:12.3: anupaṭṭhitakāyassati ca → anupaṭṭhitāya satiyāva (sya-all); anupaṭṭhitāya satiyā ca (pts1ed, mr)
